title: Hormuz Ceasefire: What 'Fragile Peace' Hides When Warships Are Already in Position
narrative: Trump and CENTCOM publicly maintain a 'fragile ceasefire' is in effect with Iran. Trump called tanker strikes 'just a love tap.' Secretary of State Rubio says US expects a 'serious' diplomatic response. China is positioned as mediator. The story in official accounts: ceasefire holds, military pressure is calibrated and limited, diplomacy is live.
telemetry: US fired on and disabled Iranian oil tankers on May 7, May 8, and May 9 (AP, CNBC, multiple sources)
Both sides exchanged fire in the Strait of Hormuz on May 8, each claiming the other fired first
The ceasefire is described as 'tattered' and 'shaky' in the same reports that quote officials saying it holds
Strait of Hormuz carries 20% of world oil trade; Hapag-Lloyd reports $60M/week in shipping losses
Oil briefly hit $100/barrel; jet fuel up 84% since February
US destroyers stationary at chokepoints, carrier positioned with full air-cap; 18% transit speed drop across Gulf corridor; zero documented convoy rendezvous
analysis: The official narrative says a ceasefire holds. The telemetry says something different. Warships don't sit stationary at chokepoints when you're escorting tankers through a corridor you claim is safe. Carriers don't position for air operations when the mission is de-escalation. The absence of any recorded convoy rendezvous — in a strait that normally sees hundreds — is itself the signal. What 'ceasefire' means in Hormuz right now is that both sides are holding position for the next move, not that the next move has been called off.
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT]: Narrative claims ceasefire holds. Warship telemetry shows both sides in combat positioning.
